Special relativity, formulated by Albert Einstein in 1905, applies to objects moving at constant speeds, especially those approaching the speed of light. It introduces the idea that the laws of physics are the same for all non-accelerating observers, and that the speed of light in a vacuum is constant, regardless of the motion of the light source or observer. One of the most famous consequences of special relativity is the phenomenon of **time dilation**, where time appears to pass more slowly for an object in motion compared to an object at rest, as observed from the rest frame. This effect becomes significant at speeds close to the speed of light.
